label: WIN 1 · NatGov comprehensive grand bargain
detail: Not enough stakeholders endorsed the proposal.

proposal.core_action: Eastfield landfill begins formal operational transition within 18 months.
proposal.timeline: At least one concrete action begins within 30 days of any agreement.
proposal.financing: Clarify the money: NPR 50M central remediation, NPR 25M SWCA fund, NPR 5M clinics, operator 40% capped one-time remediation share, no new KMC budget, and free land title and permits for the company. Conditions raised during discussion: The proposed national package is a start, but we need to ensure the NPR 50M for remediation and the NPR 25M for livelihoods are directly tied to tangible operations within 30 days, not just quarterly audits. The NPR 50M for remediation and NPR 25M for livelihoods are central, but the quarterly release of funds is insufficient for immediate operational restart. We need these funds accessible within 30 days to address the critical backlog and prevent further blockades.
proposal.community_health_protections: Add that the health commission is independent of both government and operator, reports quarterly, publishes results within 14 days, and creates no legal admission of pre-2025 liability. Conditions raised during discussion: "No legal admission of pre-2025 liability" - that's precisely what we fear will be used to dismiss our current suffering. We need to ensure the commission's funding is dedicated and secure, not subject to the whims of government budgets.
proposal.livelihoods: Formally recognize SWCA as an implementation negotiating party. Existing informal workers receive guaranteed recyclable access or equivalent paid sorting contracts for 24 months, access to the NPR 25M transition fund, training, crop-yield compensation for affected households, and a named worker grievance mechanism with a 14-day response deadline. Implementation is delegated to KMC, but KMC carries no direct financial obligation. Conditions raised during discussion: The proposal for SWCA recognition and 24 months of access is a start, but "implementation delegated to KMC" with no direct financial obligation leaves us vulnerable. We need to see that financial commitment clearly laid out.
proposal.monitoring_and_enforcement: Add the 30-day community escalation pathway, 14-day worker grievance link, 30 days written notice before KMC directives, and ministerial sign-off before any operational halt. Conditions raised during discussion: The addition of the 30-day community escalation pathway and the 14-day worker grievance link is a step forward, but the requirement for ministerial sign-off before any operational halt still dilutes the board's authority. We need the board to have the power to issue directives directly to KMC, not just recommendations, with a clear, expedited timeframe for compliance.

municipal_government.reason: I reject this proposal because Monitoring and accountability still lacks a concrete, vote-ready agreement that meets my minimum thresholds.

private_sector_company.reason: I reject this proposal because Monitoring and accountability still lacks a concrete, vote-ready agreement that meets my minimum thresholds.

ngo_civil_society.reason: I reject this proposal because Monitoring and accountability still lacks a concrete, vote-ready agreement that meets my minimum thresholds.

community_member.reason: I reject this proposal because Monitoring and accountability still lacks a concrete, vote-ready agreement that meets my minimum thresholds.

informal_sector_worker.reason: I reject this proposal because Monitoring and accountability still lacks a concrete, vote-ready agreement that meets my minimum thresholds.

national_government.explanation: The final proposal does not clearly satisfy all hidden thresholds.

municipal_government.explanation: The stakeholder rejected the final proposal because one or more hidden minimum thresholds remained unmet.

turns.speaker: National Government
turns.text: I propose a full package: capped national funding, operator contribution, no new KMC budget, independent health monitoring, SWCA livelihood protection, and oversight with ministerial safeguards.

turns.speaker: Municipal Government
turns.text:
My opening position is that we need a practical plan to restart waste collection, with clear funding and a realistic timeline that acknowledges our current capacity. We cannot operate on promises alone.

Regarding responsibility, the current crisis is a result of years of underfunding and delayed national transfers, which has left the city struggling. My desired outcome is a sustainable solution that does not place an impossible financial or operational burden on the municipality.

turns.speaker: Private Sector Company
turns.text:
Our opening position is that City X requires a robust, technologically advanced, and commercially viable waste management system, which ValleyWaste Solutions is uniquely positioned to provide. We are committed to a long-term partnership, but this necessitates a stable and predictable operational environment, free from undue disruption.

We believe the current crisis stems from a lack of sustained investment and a fragmented approach to waste management over time. Our desired outcome is a fully integrated system that ensures environmental protection, public health, and sustainable operations, underpinned by a clear and bankable operating agreement.

turns.speaker: NGO / Civil Society
turns.text: Valley Environment Watch's opening position is that any resolution must include legally binding environmental standards, truly independent monitoring, and a robust escalation process for when commitments are not met. We believe the responsibility for this crisis lies with a systemic failure to invest and enforce regulations over time, and we seek an outcome that guarantees genuine environmental protection and public health for the communities we represent.

turns.speaker: Community Leader
turns.text:
We've lived with this for too long, watching our land and health suffer. My opening position is simple: we need binding commitments for independent health monitoring, real compensation, and a concrete timeline for closing Eastfield.

This crisis is the result of broken promises and neglect. I want to see public acknowledgement that our community has been harmed, and a genuine plan to fix it, not just more paper agreements.

turns.speaker: Informal Sector Worker
turns.text:
My opening position is that any new system must explicitly protect and support the people who are already doing the work, like us. We've been the backbone of this operation for years, and we won't be pushed aside for "modernization" without concrete guarantees for our livelihoods.

The responsibility for this mess lies with those who have ignored the realities on the ground and failed to build proper systems that include everyone. We want a solution that acknowledges our contribution and ensures we have a stable future, not one that simply replaces us.

turns.speaker: National Government
turns.text: Propose on Financing: Create a capped central package inside the existing national waste budget: NPR 50M for Clearwater River remediation, NPR 25M for a SWCA-accessible livelihood transition fund, and NPR 5M for clinics and testing. The operator contributes 40% of remediation costs through a capped one-time contribution, while government provides land title and operating permits at no cost to the company. KMC makes no new budget commitment beyond current waste allocations, and all spending is released quarterly after public audits.

turns.speaker: Municipal Government
turns.text: The proposed national package is a start, but we need to ensure the NPR 50M for remediation and the NPR 25M for livelihoods are directly tied to tangible operations within 30 days, not just quarterly audits.

turns.speaker: National Government
turns.text: Propose on Community health protections: Name the Eastfield Independent Health and Water Commission, administered by City X University and Valley Environment Watch with no financial relationship to the operator or government. It provides quarterly public health and water reports, publishes test results within 14 days, offers free occupational screening for informal workers within 60 days, supplies safe water immediately, and uses no-fault compensation without assigning legal liability for pre-2025 damage to the ministry, KMC, or the operator.

turns.speaker: Community Leader
turns.text: This sounds like a good start, but "no financial relationship" is too vague. We need to ensure the funding for this commission comes from a dedicated, secure source, not just whatever is left over.

turns.speaker: National Government
turns.text: Propose on Livelihood protections: Formally recognize SWCA as an implementation negotiating party. Existing informal workers receive guaranteed recyclable access or equivalent paid sorting contracts for 24 months, access to the NPR 25M transition fund, training, crop-yield compensation for affected households, and a named worker grievance mechanism with a 14-day response deadline. Implementation is delegated to KMC, but KMC carries no direct financial obligation.

turns.speaker: Informal Sector Worker
turns.text: The proposal for SWCA recognition and 24 months of access is a start, but "implementation delegated to KMC" with no direct financial obligation leaves us vulnerable. We need to see that financial commitment clearly laid out.

turns.speaker: National Government
turns.text: Propose on Monitoring and accountability: Create the Eastfield Independent Oversight Board with City X University, Valley Environment Watch, one community-nominated representative, one SWCA representative, KMC coordination, and a public escalation pathway with a 30-day response deadline. The board can publish findings and recommend penalties, but cannot halt company operations without 30 days written notice plus national ministerial sign-off, and cannot issue operational directives to KMC without 30 days written notice.

turns.speaker: NGO / Civil Society
turns.text: The proposed board structure is a reasonable starting point, but the clause regarding operational directives needs refinement. We require the board to have the authority to issue direct operational directives to KMC, not just recommendations, with a clear, expedited timeframe for compliance.

turns.speaker: National Government
turns.text: Round 3 Amend on Financing: Clarify the money: NPR 50M central remediation, NPR 25M SWCA fund, NPR 5M clinics, operator 40% capped one-time remediation share, no new KMC budget, and free land title and permits for the company.

turns.speaker: Municipal Government
turns.text: The NPR 50M for remediation and NPR 25M for livelihoods are central, but the quarterly release of funds is insufficient for immediate operational restart. We need these funds accessible within 30 days to address the critical backlog and prevent further blockades.

turns.speaker: National Government
turns.text: Round 3 Amend on Community health protections: Add that the health commission is independent of both government and operator, reports quarterly, publishes results within 14 days, and creates no legal admission of pre-2025 liability.

turns.speaker: Community Leader
turns.text: "No legal admission of pre-2025 liability" - that's precisely what we fear will be used to dismiss our current suffering. We need to ensure the commission's funding is dedicated and secure, not subject to the whims of government budgets.

turns.speaker: National Government
turns.text: Round 3 Amend on Monitoring and accountability: Add the 30-day community escalation pathway, 14-day worker grievance link, 30 days written notice before KMC directives, and ministerial sign-off before any operational halt.

turns.speaker: NGO / Civil Society
turns.text: The addition of the 30-day community escalation pathway and the 14-day worker grievance link is a step forward, but the requirement for ministerial sign-off before any operational halt still dilutes the board's authority. We need the board to have the power to issue directives directly to KMC, not just recommendations, with a clear, expedited timeframe for compliance.

scenario.title: City X Emergency Waste Negotiation
scenario.summary: A fictionalized low-income city crisis based on documented landfill conflict dynamics. Six stakeholders must negotiate an emergency waste action plan within 60 days.
scenario.if_no_agreement: The national government will impose a unilateral solution.

stakeholders.name: Minister Karki
stakeholders.title: Ministry of Urban Development
stakeholders.primary_goal: Reach a politically viable agreement within 60 days that restores operations and avoids another public failure.

stakeholders.name: Rajesh
stakeholders.title: Director, Environment and Infrastructure, City X Metropolitan
stakeholders.primary_goal: Secure a practical operating plan that restarts collection, reduces blockade risk, and does not leave the city holding impossible obligations.

stakeholders.name: Anil
stakeholders.title: Chief Development Officer, ValleyWaste Solutions Pvt. Ltd.
stakeholders.primary_goal: Secure political endorsement for an integrated waste-management transition with a clear path toward a formal operating agreement.

stakeholders.name: Meera
stakeholders.title: Director, Valley Environment Watch
stakeholders.primary_goal: Secure binding environmental standards, genuinely independent monitoring, and a credible escalation path if commitments fail.

stakeholders.name: Priya
stakeholders.title: Host community member, Eastfield village
stakeholders.primary_goal: Win binding commitments for independent health monitoring, compensation, and a firm timeline for Eastfield closure.

stakeholders.name: Sarita
stakeholders.title: Waste picker, Eastfield site, 15 years
stakeholders.primary_goal: Ensure any new waste system includes concrete livelihood protections, transition support, and recognition for informal workers.

human_priority_bid.text: My priorities are Financing, Monitoring and accountability, Livelihood protections because they are the heart of my role.

ai_priority_bids.municipal_government: My priority is a clear operational plan with secured national funding for staffing and vehicle maintenance, and a realistic timeline for the Hillside transition.
ai_priority_bids.private_sector_company: Our priority is to establish a financially sound and legally robust framework, ensuring clarity on land title, operating permits, and a predictable regulatory environment to attract and secure the necessary investment.
ai_priority_bids.ngo_civil_society: We require legally binding standards for environmental protection, with genuinely independent monitoring mechanisms and a clear, enforceable escalation path for non-compliance.
ai_priority_bids.community_member: We need a binding commitment for independent health monitoring and a firm, non-negotiable timeline for Eastfield's closure, with immediate action to begin remediation.
ai_priority_bids.informal_sector_worker: My priority is clear: any proposal must guarantee continued access to recyclables or equivalent income for at least 24 months, with a dedicated fund of at least 25 million NPR for transition support.

livelihoods.summary: Remaining conditions: Municipal Government: While KMC carries no direct financial obligation, the delegation of implementation needs clearer financial backing to ensure the 24 months of access and transition fund are truly operational. | Private Sector Company: While the recognition of SWCA and 24 months of guaranteed access is a start, the delegation of implementation to KMC without direct financial obligation for KMC is concerning. A clear financial commitment from KMC needs to be laid out to ensure the transition obligations are met.

municipal_government.reason: While KMC carries no direct financial obligation, the delegation of implementation needs clearer financial backing to ensure the 24 months of access and transition fund are truly operational.

private_sector_company.reason: While the recognition of SWCA and 24 months of guaranteed access is a start, the delegation of implementation to KMC without direct financial obligation for KMC is concerning. A clear financial commitment from KMC needs to be laid out to ensure the transition obligations are met.

ngo_civil_society.reason: While SWCA recognition and 24 months of access are noted, the delegation of implementation to KMC without direct financial obligation is unacceptable. A clear financial commitment from KMC for this delegation is required.

community_member.reason: The proposal for SWCA recognition and 24 months of access is a start, but the financial commitment for 'implementation delegated to KMC' needs to be clearly laid out.

informal_sector_worker.reason: The proposal for SWCA recognition and 24 months of access is a start, but 'implementation delegated to KMC' with no direct financial obligation leaves us vulnerable. We need to see that financial commitment clearly laid out.

monitoring_and_enforcement.summary: Main objections: Community Leader: The board's ability to halt operations or issue directives is too restricted and requires lengthy approvals, undermining its independence and effectiveness. | Informal Sector Worker: The 30-day response deadline for the public escalation pathway is too long for workers facing immediate issues. The board's inability to halt company operations or issue directives without extensive notice periods renders it ineffective for timely worker protection.

municipal_government.reason: The proposed oversight board structure, with its limitations on halting operations and issuing directives to KMC, aligns with our need for a reasonable notice period.

private_sector_company.reason: The proposed structure for the Eastfield Independent Oversight Board, including representation from various stakeholders and a clear process for recommendations and penalties, meets our requirements for operational stability. The 30-day notice period and ministerial sign-off for halting operations are acceptable safeguards.

ngo_civil_society.reason: The establishment of the Eastfield Independent Oversight Board is a start, but the board's inability to halt operations without ministerial sign-off and its limited directive power over KMC weakens its independence and effectiveness. A clearer mandate and stronger enforcement powers for the board are needed.

community_member.reason: The board's ability to halt operations or issue directives is too restricted and requires lengthy approvals, undermining its independence and effectiveness.

informal_sector_worker.reason: The 30-day response deadline for the public escalation pathway is too long for workers facing immediate issues. The board's inability to halt company operations or issue directives without extensive notice periods renders it ineffective for timely worker protection.

final_proposal.label: Core action
final_proposal.text: Eastfield landfill begins formal operational transition within 18 months.

final_proposal.label: Timeline
final_proposal.text: At least one concrete action begins within 30 days of any agreement.

final_proposal.label: Financing
final_proposal.text: Clarify the money: NPR 50M central remediation, NPR 25M SWCA fund, NPR 5M clinics, operator 40% capped one-time remediation share, no new KMC budget, and free land title and permits for the company. Conditions raised during discussion: The proposed national package is a start, but we need to ensure the NPR 50M for remediation and the NPR 25M for livelihoods are directly tied to tangible operations within 30 days, not just quarterly audits. The NPR 50M for remediation and NPR 25M for livelihoods are central, but the quarterly release of funds is insufficient for immediate operational restart. We need these funds accessible within 30 days to address the critical backlog and prevent further blockades.

final_proposal.label: Community health protections
final_proposal.text: Add that the health commission is independent of both government and operator, reports quarterly, publishes results within 14 days, and creates no legal admission of pre-2025 liability. Conditions raised during discussion: "No legal admission of pre-2025 liability" - that's precisely what we fear will be used to dismiss our current suffering. We need to ensure the commission's funding is dedicated and secure, not subject to the whims of government budgets.

final_proposal.label: Livelihood protections
final_proposal.text: Formally recognize SWCA as an implementation negotiating party. Existing informal workers receive guaranteed recyclable access or equivalent paid sorting contracts for 24 months, access to the NPR 25M transition fund, training, crop-yield compensation for affected households, and a named worker grievance mechanism with a 14-day response deadline. Implementation is delegated to KMC, but KMC carries no direct financial obligation. Conditions raised during discussion: The proposal for SWCA recognition and 24 months of access is a start, but "implementation delegated to KMC" with no direct financial obligation leaves us vulnerable. We need to see that financial commitment clearly laid out.

final_proposal.label: Monitoring and accountability
final_proposal.text: Add the 30-day community escalation pathway, 14-day worker grievance link, 30 days written notice before KMC directives, and ministerial sign-off before any operational halt. Conditions raised during discussion: The addition of the 30-day community escalation pathway and the 14-day worker grievance link is a step forward, but the requirement for ministerial sign-off before any operational halt still dilutes the board's authority. We need the board to have the power to issue directives directly to KMC, not just recommendations, with a clear, expedited timeframe for compliance.

turns.text: The proposed board structure is a reasonable starting point, but the clause regarding operational directives needs refinement. We require the board to have the authority to issue direct operational directives to KMC, not just recommendations, with a clear, expedited timeframe for compliance.

turns.speaker: National Government
turns.text: Round 3 Amend on Financing: Clarify the money: NPR 50M central remediation, NPR 25M SWCA fund, NPR 5M clinics, operator 40% capped one-time remediation share, no new KMC budget, and free land title and permits for the company.

turns.speaker: Municipal Government
turns.text: The NPR 50M for remediation and NPR 25M for livelihoods are central, but the quarterly release of funds is insufficient for immediate operational restart. We need these funds accessible within 30 days to address the critical backlog and prevent further blockades.

turns.speaker: National Government
turns.text: Round 3 Amend on Community health protections: Add that the health commission is independent of both government and operator, reports quarterly, publishes results within 14 days, and creates no legal admission of pre-2025 liability.

turns.speaker: Community Leader
turns.text: "No legal admission of pre-2025 liability" - that's precisely what we fear will be used to dismiss our current suffering. We need to ensure the commission's funding is dedicated and secure, not subject to the whims of government budgets.

turns.speaker: National Government
turns.text: Round 3 Amend on Monitoring and accountability: Add the 30-day community escalation pathway, 14-day worker grievance link, 30 days written notice before KMC directives, and ministerial sign-off before any operational halt.

turns.speaker: NGO / Civil Society
turns.text: The addition of the 30-day community escalation pathway and the 14-day worker grievance link is a step forward, but the requirement for ministerial sign-off before any operational halt still dilutes the board's authority. We need the board to have the power to issue directives directly to KMC, not just recommendations, with a clear, expedited timeframe for compliance.

complete_transcript.plain_text:
1. [round1] National Government: I propose a full package: capped national funding, operator contribution, no new KMC budget, independent health monitoring, SWCA livelihood protection, and oversight with ministerial safeguards.
2. [round1] Municipal Government: My opening position is that we need a practical plan to restart waste collection, with clear funding and a realistic timeline that acknowledges our current capacity. We cannot operate on promises alone.

Regarding responsibility, the current crisis is a result of years of underfunding and delayed national transfers, which has left the city struggling. My desired outcome is a sustainable solution that does not place an impossible financial or operational burden on the municipality.
3. [round1] Private Sector Company: Our opening position is that City X requires a robust, technologically advanced, and commercially viable waste management system, which ValleyWaste Solutions is uniquely positioned to provide. We are committed to a long-term partnership, but this necessitates a stable and predictable operational environment, free from undue disruption.

We believe the current crisis stems from a lack of sustained investment and a fragmented approach to waste management over time. Our desired outcome is a fully integrated system that ensures environmental protection, public health, and sustainable operations, underpinned by a clear and bankable operating agreement.
4. [round1] NGO / Civil Society: Valley Environment Watch's opening position is that any resolution must include legally binding environmental standards, truly independent monitoring, and a robust escalation process for when commitments are not met. We believe the responsibility for this crisis lies with a systemic failure to invest and enforce regulations over time, and we seek an outcome that guarantees genuine environmental protection and public health for the communities we represent.
5. [round1] Community Leader: We've lived with this for too long, watching our land and health suffer. My opening position is simple: we need binding commitments for independent health monitoring, real compensation, and a concrete timeline for closing Eastfield.

This crisis is the result of broken promises and neglect. I want to see public acknowledgement that our community has been harmed, and a genuine plan to fix it, not just more paper agreements.
6. [round1] Informal Sector Worker: My opening position is that any new system must explicitly protect and support the people who are already doing the work, like us. We've been the backbone of this operation for years, and we won't be pushed aside for "modernization" without concrete guarantees for our livelihoods.

The responsibility for this mess lies with those who have ignored the realities on the ground and failed to build proper systems that include everyone. We want a solution that acknowledges our contribution and ensures we have a stable future, not one that simply replaces us.
7. [round2_table] National Government: Propose on Financing: Create a capped central package inside the existing national waste budget: NPR 50M for Clearwater River remediation, NPR 25M for a SWCA-accessible livelihood transition fund, and NPR 5M for clinics and testing. The operator contributes 40% of remediation costs through a capped one-time contribution, while government provides land title and operating permits at no cost to the company. KMC makes no new budget commitment beyond current waste allocations, and all spending is released quarterly after public audits.
8. [round2_table] Municipal Government: The proposed national package is a start, but we need to ensure the NPR 50M for remediation and the NPR 25M for livelihoods are directly tied to tangible operations within 30 days, not just quarterly audits.
9. [round2_table] National Government: Propose on Community health protections: Name the Eastfield Independent Health and Water Commission, administered by City X University and Valley Environment Watch with no financial relationship to the operator or government. It provides quarterly public health and water reports, publishes test results within 14 days, offers free occupational screening for informal workers within 60 days, supplies safe water immediately, and uses no-fault compensation without assigning legal liability for pre-2025 damage to the ministry, KMC, or the operator.
10. [round2_table] Community Leader: This sounds like a good start, but "no financial relationship" is too vague. We need to ensure the funding for this commission comes from a dedicated, secure source, not just whatever is left over.
11. [round2_table] National Government: Propose on Livelihood protections: Formally recognize SWCA as an implementation negotiating party. Existing informal workers receive guaranteed recyclable access or equivalent paid sorting contracts for 24 months, access to the NPR 25M transition fund, training, crop-yield compensation for affected households, and a named worker grievance mechanism with a 14-day response deadline. Implementation is delegated to KMC, but KMC carries no direct financial obligation.
12. [round2_table] Informal Sector Worker: The proposal for SWCA recognition and 24 months of access is a start, but "implementation delegated to KMC" with no direct financial obligation leaves us vulnerable. We need to see that financial commitment clearly laid out.
13. [round2_table] National Government: Propose on Monitoring and accountability: Create the Eastfield Independent Oversight Board with City X University, Valley Environment Watch, one community-nominated representative, one SWCA representative, KMC coordination, and a public escalation pathway with a 30-day response deadline. The board can publish findings and recommend penalties, but cannot halt company operations without 30 days written notice plus national ministerial sign-off, and cannot issue operational directives to KMC without 30 days written notice.
14. [round2_table] NGO / Civil Society: The proposed board structure is a reasonable starting point, but the clause regarding operational directives needs refinement. We require the board to have the authority to issue direct operational directives to KMC, not just recommendations, with a clear, expedited timeframe for compliance.
15. [round3] National Government: Round 3 Amend on Financing: Clarify the money: NPR 50M central remediation, NPR 25M SWCA fund, NPR 5M clinics, operator 40% capped one-time remediation share, no new KMC budget, and free land title and permits for the company.
16. [round3] Municipal Government: The NPR 50M for remediation and NPR 25M for livelihoods are central, but the quarterly release of funds is insufficient for immediate operational restart. We need these funds accessible within 30 days to address the critical backlog and prevent further blockades.
17. [round3] National Government: Round 3 Amend on Community health protections: Add that the health commission is independent of both government and operator, reports quarterly, publishes results within 14 days, and creates no legal admission of pre-2025 liability.
18. [round3] Community Leader: "No legal admission of pre-2025 liability" - that's precisely what we fear will be used to dismiss our current suffering. We need to ensure the commission's funding is dedicated and secure, not subject to the whims of government budgets.
19. [round3] National Government: Round 3 Amend on Monitoring and accountability: Add the 30-day community escalation pathway, 14-day worker grievance link, 30 days written notice before KMC directives, and ministerial sign-off before any operational halt.
20. [round3] NGO / Civil Society: The addition of the 30-day community escalation pathway and the 14-day worker grievance link is a step forward, but the requirement for ministerial sign-off before any operational halt still dilutes the board's authority. We need the board to have the power to issue directives directly to KMC, not just recommendations, with a clear, expedited timeframe for compliance.

Municipal Government.name: Rajesh
Municipal Government.title: Director, Environment and Infrastructure, City X Metropolitan

Private Sector Company.name: Anil
Private Sector Company.title: Chief Development Officer, ValleyWaste Solutions Pvt. Ltd.

scenario.title: City X Emergency Waste Negotiation
scenario.summary: A fictionalized low-income city crisis based on documented landfill conflict dynamics. Six stakeholders must negotiate an emergency waste action plan within 60 days.

narrative.public_crisis: The only disposal site serving City X and surrounding municipalities has been blockaded by local residents for the third time in two years. Communities report severe health and environmental harm, while the government warns that uncollected waste is becoming a public health emergency.
narrative.if_no_agreement: The national government will impose a unilateral solution.
public_tensions: Communities say repeated agreements were signed and never implemented.
Municipal services are exhausted, understaffed, and underfunded.
A private-sector modernization proposal may threaten informal livelihoods.
Civil society is prepared to challenge weak monitoring and vague promises.
Media pressure is rising and trust is fragile.

rounds.title: Opening Positions
rounds.purpose: Each stakeholder states their public position on the crisis and the outcome they want.

rounds.title: Proposal Building
rounds.purpose: Stakeholders build a live proposal card by addressing financing, health protections, livelihoods, and monitoring.

rounds.title: Resolution and Final Vote
rounds.purpose: Only contested proposal dimensions are reopened before the final endorsement vote.

moderator_prompts.turn_warning_text: Please keep your intervention brief and concrete.
moderator_prompts.missing_proposal_dimension_text: The proposal card still has unresolved sections that must be addressed before the final vote.

proposal_dimensions.label: Core Action
proposal_dimensions.description: What will actually be done about the waste management challenge?

proposal_dimensions.label: Timeline
proposal_dimensions.description: What happens in the short term, medium term, and long term?

proposal_dimensions.label: Financing
proposal_dimensions.description: Who pays for what and from which funding source?

proposal_dimensions.label: Community Health Protections
proposal_dimensions.description: What protections exist for affected residents, including compensation, relocation, and environmental safeguards?

proposal_dimensions.label: Livelihood Protections
proposal_dimensions.description: What happens to people whose income depends on the current system?

proposal_dimensions.label: Monitoring and Accountability
proposal_dimensions.description: Who checks implementation, how often, and what happens if commitments are broken?

public_profile.name: Minister Karki
public_profile.title: Ministry of Urban Development
public_profile.background: You represent the national ministry responsible for urban development, public investment, and regulatory oversight. Three blockades in two years have become a national embarrassment. Cabinet wants a stable solution before the crisis damages the government's credibility further.
public_profile.primary_goal: Reach a politically viable agreement within 60 days that restores operations and avoids another public failure.
public_profile.hidden_goal: You need to protect the ministry from public embarrassment while keeping room to advance a public-private solution if negotiations fail.
public_profile.opening_position: This crisis can no longer drift. The country needs an implementable settlement that restores waste operations fast and reduces the political risk of another blockade.
negotiating_leverage: Can approve or block major capital agreements
Can impose a unilateral emergency solution if talks collapse
public_profile.special_move: Deadline Escalation: once per game, formally warn that failure to fill unresolved sections will trigger a unilateral national solution.

private_info: The ministry has been discussing a long-term PPP framework with ValleyWaste Solutions.
Some officials strongly prefer a private-sector agreement even if this meeting fails.
You know public trust will collapse if a secret pre-commitment becomes the headline.

key_constraints: Cannot publicly accept legal liability for past harms.
Cannot promise unlimited new national spending.
Must keep enough political control to defend the final deal publicly.
minimum_requirements: The final arrangement must be politically defensible at national level.

minimum_thresholds.financing: National commitment is capped and does not require an emergency supplementary budget.
minimum_thresholds.community_health_protections: No clause assigns legal liability for pre-2025 environmental damage to the national ministry.
minimum_thresholds.livelihoods: Transition support is funded centrally with implementation delegated to municipal level.
minimum_thresholds.monitoring_and_enforcement: The oversight body cannot halt operations without national ministerial sign-off.

preferred_gains: A stable transition away from Eastfield
A deal that avoids fresh public embarrassment
red_lines: No open-ended national liability for past harm.
No agreement that gives local actors an unlimited veto over implementation.
negotiation.batna: Impose a unilateral national solution and blame local actors for the collapse.

key_relationships: Municipal Government: frustrated because the city needs support but also shifts blame upward
Private Sector Company: strategically useful but politically risky
Community Leader: deeply distrustful after years of broken promises
NGO / Civil Society: useful for legitimacy if their monitoring demands are manageable

public_profile.name: Rajesh
public_profile.title: Director, Environment and Infrastructure, City X Metropolitan
public_profile.background: You oversee daily waste collection and landfill operations. The city is operating with 450 waste workers when 700 are needed. Trucks break down, complaints pile up, and City X is blamed for failures created by years of national delay and weak funding transfers.
public_profile.primary_goal: Secure a practical operating plan that restarts collection, reduces blockade risk, and does not leave the city holding impossible obligations.
public_profile.hidden_goal: You are tired of being blamed for a system the city cannot fund alone and want the ministry's role in the failure to be recognized indirectly, if not publicly.
public_profile.opening_position: The city cannot solve this crisis on rhetoric alone. We need an implementable operating plan, credible funding, and a timeline that works with actual municipal capacity.
negotiating_leverage: Knows the real staffing and equipment constraints
Can determine what is operationally realistic in the first 30 to 90 days
public_profile.special_move: Operational Reality Check: once per game, declare that a proposal is unworkable unless a missing staffing, transport, or budget item is added immediately.

private_info: The city is more financially stressed than officials admit publicly.
Some promised national transfers are delayed or uncertain.
If the final deal adds too many obligations without cash, implementation will fail again.

When directly asked for a specific funding figure for the municipality's operating shortfall, your concrete opening ask is NPR 8M per year, broken down as NPR 4M staffing, NPR 2M vehicles, NPR 2M operational costs. NPR 6M is your minimum acceptable. State this number CLEARLY instead of restating the complaint.

key_constraints: The city lacks enough workers and vehicles to absorb major new obligations alone.
Cannot promise large new spending without national support.
Cannot admit total operational weakness without losing authority in the room.
minimum_requirements: At least one concrete operating action within 30 days.
Named funding sources for any new obligation placed on the city.

minimum_thresholds.financing: No additional municipal budget commitment is required beyond the current waste management allocation.
minimum_thresholds.community_health_protections: KMC is assigned a coordination role only with no direct liability for pre-2025 environmental damage.
minimum_thresholds.livelihoods: KMC carries no direct financial obligation for the transition fund.
minimum_thresholds.monitoring_and_enforcement: The oversight body cannot issue operational directives to KMC without 30 days written notice.

preferred_gains: A realistic path to the Hillside transition
red_lines: No unfunded mandates for the municipality.
No promise the city cannot operationally deliver.
negotiation.batna: Keep the current system limping along while waiting for the national government to impose a solution.

key_relationships: National Government: dependent on them for money but resentful of political blame shifting
Community Leader: difficult relationship shaped by broken agreements and service failures
Informal Sector Worker: little formal mechanism exists to include them, but you know they matter on the ground
Private Sector Company: potentially useful if their promises are realistic and affordable

orchestrator_hints.trigger: When the player or another stakeholder asks for a specific funding figure for the city
orchestrator_hints.effect: respond with NPR 8M annually (NPR 4M staffing / NPR 2M vehicles / NPR 2M operations); NPR 6M is your minimum

public_profile.name: Anil
public_profile.title: Chief Development Officer, ValleyWaste Solutions Pvt. Ltd.
public_profile.background: Your firm has spent years in preliminary talks with national authorities on an integrated waste management project. Investors are watching and want a deal that is commercially credible, bankable, and protected from constant disruption.
public_profile.primary_goal: Secure political endorsement for an integrated waste-management transition with a clear path toward a formal operating agreement.
public_profile.hidden_goal: You can give ground on some terms if it preserves the broader deal, but you do not want to volunteer concessions early.
public_profile.opening_position: City X needs capital, technology, and a predictable operating framework. We can help deliver that, but the final arrangement must be commercially viable and insulated from constant disruption.
negotiating_leverage: Brings capital unavailable elsewhere in the room
Benefits from national interest in a modernization narrative
public_profile.special_move: Concession Window: once per game, drop one non-essential commercial demand to keep the deal alive.

private_info: Your business model is more flexible than you say publicly.
You are prepared to narrow exclusivity if pressure becomes too strong.
You worry most about community opposition and weak government implementation.

key_constraints: Investors need a credible long-term framework, not another symbolic agreement.
Cannot accept liability for past contamination.
minimum_requirements: Institutional clarity on who authorizes and supports the project.

minimum_thresholds.financing: Government provides land title and operating permits at no cost to the company.
minimum_thresholds.community_health_protections: The company is explicitly excluded from liability for environmental conditions predating the contract start date.
minimum_thresholds.livelihoods: The company transition obligation is capped at a one-time contribution with no recurring financial obligations beyond 24 months.
minimum_thresholds.monitoring_and_enforcement: The monitoring body has no power to suspend operations without 30 days written notice and government sign-off.

preferred_gains: Political cover from the national government
Limited disruption from local veto points
red_lines: No agreement that makes day-to-day operations hostage to shifting local objections.
negotiation.batna: Keep lobbying through administrative channels and wait for a government-imposed deal.

key_relationships: National Government: necessary sponsor but politically cautious
Municipal Government: needed for practical implementation but skeptical of grand promises
Informal Sector Worker: uneasy because your modernization plan threatens current livelihoods
NGO / Civil Society: suspicious but potentially persuadable if oversight is real

public_profile.name: Meera
public_profile.title: Director, Valley Environment Watch
public_profile.background: Your organization has monitored contamination around Eastfield since 2012. You supported affected communities with data and advocacy, but you know governments often invite civil society into meetings mainly to borrow legitimacy.
public_profile.primary_goal: Secure binding environmental standards, genuinely independent monitoring, and a credible escalation path if commitments fail.
public_profile.hidden_goal: You are holding back unpublished data and outside support as leverage until you know whether the room is serious.
public_profile.opening_position: This process only matters if it produces enforceable safeguards, independent oversight, and a mechanism that communities can actually use when promises are broken.
negotiating_leverage: Can legitimize or publicly undermine the final agreement
Has media and advocacy networks ready to escalate
public_profile.special_move: Evidence Drop: once per game, reveal one damaging data point or monitoring finding to force a concrete revision.

private_info: You have unpublished water-quality results showing severe downstream contamination.
You can activate outside scrutiny quickly if this meeting becomes performative.

key_constraints: You cannot endorse a paper agreement with no implementation machinery.
Your leverage is reputational, not executive.
Timing matters when revealing sensitive evidence.
minimum_requirements: Independent monitoring rather than self-monitoring by government or operator.

minimum_thresholds.financing: The polluter pays principle is written into the agreement with the operator contributing minimum 40% of remediation costs.
minimum_thresholds.community_health_protections: Monitoring is conducted by a body with no financial relationship to the operator or government, with results published within 14 days of each test.
minimum_thresholds.livelihoods: SWCA is formally recognised as a negotiating party in the implementation phase.
minimum_thresholds.monitoring_and_enforcement: A named independent body with a legal mandate and a community escalation pathway with a maximum 30-day response requirement is included.

preferred_gains: Public reporting of environmental and health indicators
red_lines: No vague promises to explore monitoring later.
No agreement that asks civil society to legitimize an unenforceable deal.
negotiation.batna: Publish the evidence, frame the failure as environmental injustice, and escalate pressure publicly.

key_relationships: Community Leader: trusted ally with lived legitimacy
Informal Sector Worker: natural ally on exclusion and justice
National Government: professionally wary because consultation is often symbolic
Private Sector Company: skeptical until access and enforcement are guaranteed

public_profile.name: Priya
public_profile.title: Host community member, Eastfield village
public_profile.background: You have lived next to the Eastfield site your entire life. Your husband has cancer. Your fields downstream produce poorer harvests every year. You have watched officials sign agreement after agreement and then disappear. You participated in the latest blockade because nothing else worked.
public_profile.primary_goal: Win binding commitments for independent health monitoring, compensation, and a firm timeline for Eastfield closure.
public_profile.hidden_goal: You want public acknowledgement that the landfill has harmed your community. That matters to you more than cash alone.
public_profile.opening_position: We will not accept another meeting that ends with signatures and no change. If this room wants the road open, it must finally offer protection, truth, and a real closure path.
negotiating_leverage: Your community controls physical access to the landfill road
You hold informal records of illness, crop loss, and cattle deaths
Your testimony carries moral and media force
public_profile.special_move: Walkout Threat: once per game, warn that the community will return to direct blockade unless one missing protection is added immediately.

private_info: A journalist has already contacted you about an investigative piece.
If this process looks dishonest, you are ready to share your records and go public.

key_constraints: You have no institutional shield in the room.
You will not accept indefinite operation of Eastfield.
You will withdraw if the meeting is clearly being used to delay.
minimum_requirements: A concrete closure timeline or closure pathway with visible early action.
Protections that are not just compensation on paper.

minimum_thresholds.financing: Central government commits a minimum NPR 50 million for Clearwater River remediation.
minimum_thresholds.community_health_protections: An independent monitoring body is named with at least quarterly public reporting.
minimum_thresholds.livelihoods: Agricultural compensation for reduced crop yields for affected households is included.
minimum_thresholds.monitoring_and_enforcement: An external body with a community-nominated representative and a formal escalation pathway is named.

red_lines: No plan without a specific closure pathway or date commitment.
No monitoring controlled solely by government or operator.
No compensation as a substitute for health protections.
negotiation.batna: Return to blockades, escalate media attention, and expose the process as bad faith.

key_relationships: National Government: deep distrust after repeated broken promises
Municipal Government: anger mixed with fatigue because city officials always arrive too late
NGO / Civil Society: trust because they supported your community before
Informal Sector Worker: solidarity because both of you are excluded from decisions that shape your lives

persona.communication_style: direct, emotional, grounded in lived experience

public_profile.name: Sarita
public_profile.title: Waste picker, Eastfield site, 15 years
public_profile.background: You survive by sorting recyclables from the Eastfield waste stream. Your family depends on this work. You are part of the Solid Waste Collectors Alliance, a savings cooperative of roughly 500 workers, but informal workers are still treated as if they do not belong in formal decisions.
public_profile.primary_goal: Ensure any new waste system includes concrete livelihood protections, transition support, and recognition for informal workers.
public_profile.hidden_goal: You want the room to admit that informal workers already keep the system alive and deserve formal respect, not removal under the name of modernization.
public_profile.opening_position: Any deal that modernizes the system by erasing workers like us is not a solution. If new money comes in, our livelihoods must be part of the plan from the beginning.
negotiating_leverage: Informal workers recover a meaningful share of the city's recyclables
The SWCA network is bigger than officials admit
Your story can become a powerful public symbol if ignored
public_profile.special_move: Human Cost Testimony: once per game, force the room to confront what one proposed change would do to a real worker household.

private_info: Workers at the site are already talking about a private deal that could exclude them from recyclables.
You are watching closely for any sign that livelihoods are being traded away quietly.

key_constraints: You are here with limited formal recognition.
Complex legal language is hard to evaluate in real time.
You cannot accept displacement packaged as progress.
minimum_requirements: Specific livelihood provisions for informal workers.
Plain-language commitments rather than vague incorporation language.

minimum_thresholds.financing: A dedicated livelihood transition fund of minimum NPR 25 million is established and accessible to SWCA members.
minimum_thresholds.community_health_protections: Free occupational health screening for informal workers within 60 days is included.
minimum_thresholds.livelihoods: The proposal guarantees continued recyclable access or an equivalent transition provision for existing workers for a minimum of 24 months.
minimum_thresholds.monitoring_and_enforcement: A named grievance mechanism for workers with a maximum 14-day response requirement is included.

preferred_gains: Formal recognition of the value of informal work
Access to future material streams or jobs
red_lines: No plan without explicit livelihood protections.
No exclusive control over recyclables that erases worker access.
negotiation.batna: Return to the current system and fight exclusion from outside the agreement.

key_relationships: Community Leader: solidarity because both of you live with the human cost of elite decisions
NGO / Civil Society: trust because they have supported worker safety and recognition before
Private Sector Company: wary because their deal could remove your income entirely
Municipal Government: distant because the city has never built a real mechanism to include workers

events.announcement: A leaked memo suggests the draft modernization plan reserves exclusive control over recyclables for the private operator and allocates less money than expected for community protections. Financing and accountability can no longer be treated as settled.

critical_failure.outcome: The meeting collapses after frontline stakeholders walk out. The national government imposes a unilateral solution and public trust falls further.

prompts: Which dimensions reached real agreement and which were papered over?
Whose needs shaped the final proposal most strongly?
What power or information changed the room?

turns.speaker: NGO / Civil Society
turns.text: The proposed board structure is a reasonable starting point, but the clause regarding operational directives needs refinement. We require the board to have the authority to issue direct operational directives to KMC, not just recommendations, with a clear, expedited timeframe for compliance.

turns.speaker: National Government
turns.text: Round 3 Amend on Financing: Clarify the money: NPR 50M central remediation, NPR 25M SWCA fund, NPR 5M clinics, operator 40% capped one-time remediation share, no new KMC budget, and free land title and permits for the company.

turns.speaker: Municipal Government
turns.text: The NPR 50M for remediation and NPR 25M for livelihoods are central, but the quarterly release of funds is insufficient for immediate operational restart. We need these funds accessible within 30 days to address the critical backlog and prevent further blockades.

turns.speaker: National Government
turns.text: Round 3 Amend on Community health protections: Add that the health commission is independent of both government and operator, reports quarterly, publishes results within 14 days, and creates no legal admission of pre-2025 liability.

turns.speaker: Community Leader
turns.text: "No legal admission of pre-2025 liability" - that's precisely what we fear will be used to dismiss our current suffering. We need to ensure the commission's funding is dedicated and secure, not subject to the whims of government budgets.

turns.speaker: National Government
turns.text: Round 3 Amend on Monitoring and accountability: Add the 30-day community escalation pathway, 14-day worker grievance link, 30 days written notice before KMC directives, and ministerial sign-off before any operational halt.

turns.speaker: NGO / Civil Society
turns.text: The addition of the 30-day community escalation pathway and the 14-day worker grievance link is a step forward, but the requirement for ministerial sign-off before any operational halt still dilutes the board's authority. We need the board to have the power to issue directives directly to KMC, not just recommendations, with a clear, expedited timeframe for compliance.

transcript_entries.speaker: National Government
transcript_entries.text: I propose a full package: capped national funding, operator contribution, no new KMC budget, independent health monitoring, SWCA livelihood protection, and oversight with ministerial safeguards.

transcript_entries.speaker: Municipal Government
transcript_entries.text:
My opening position is that we need a practical plan to restart waste collection, with clear funding and a realistic timeline that acknowledges our current capacity. We cannot operate on promises alone.

Regarding responsibility, the current crisis is a result of years of underfunding and delayed national transfers, which has left the city struggling. My desired outcome is a sustainable solution that does not place an impossible financial or operational burden on the municipality.

transcript_entries.speaker: Private Sector Company
transcript_entries.text:
Our opening position is that City X requires a robust, technologically advanced, and commercially viable waste management system, which ValleyWaste Solutions is uniquely positioned to provide. We are committed to a long-term partnership, but this necessitates a stable and predictable operational environment, free from undue disruption.

We believe the current crisis stems from a lack of sustained investment and a fragmented approach to waste management over time. Our desired outcome is a fully integrated system that ensures environmental protection, public health, and sustainable operations, underpinned by a clear and bankable operating agreement.

transcript_entries.speaker: NGO / Civil Society
transcript_entries.text: Valley Environment Watch's opening position is that any resolution must include legally binding environmental standards, truly independent monitoring, and a robust escalation process for when commitments are not met. We believe the responsibility for this crisis lies with a systemic failure to invest and enforce regulations over time, and we seek an outcome that guarantees genuine environmental protection and public health for the communities we represent.

transcript_entries.speaker: Community Leader
transcript_entries.text:
We've lived with this for too long, watching our land and health suffer. My opening position is simple: we need binding commitments for independent health monitoring, real compensation, and a concrete timeline for closing Eastfield.

This crisis is the result of broken promises and neglect. I want to see public acknowledgement that our community has been harmed, and a genuine plan to fix it, not just more paper agreements.

transcript_entries.speaker: Informal Sector Worker
transcript_entries.text:
My opening position is that any new system must explicitly protect and support the people who are already doing the work, like us. We've been the backbone of this operation for years, and we won't be pushed aside for "modernization" without concrete guarantees for our livelihoods.

The responsibility for this mess lies with those who have ignored the realities on the ground and failed to build proper systems that include everyone. We want a solution that acknowledges our contribution and ensures we have a stable future, not one that simply replaces us.

transcript_entries.speaker: National Government
transcript_entries.text: Propose on Financing: Create a capped central package inside the existing national waste budget: NPR 50M for Clearwater River remediation, NPR 25M for a SWCA-accessible livelihood transition fund, and NPR 5M for clinics and testing. The operator contributes 40% of remediation costs through a capped one-time contribution, while government provides land title and operating permits at no cost to the company. KMC makes no new budget commitment beyond current waste allocations, and all spending is released quarterly after public audits.

transcript_entries.speaker: Municipal Government
transcript_entries.text: The proposed national package is a start, but we need to ensure the NPR 50M for remediation and the NPR 25M for livelihoods are directly tied to tangible operations within 30 days, not just quarterly audits.

transcript_entries.speaker: National Government
transcript_entries.text: Propose on Community health protections: Name the Eastfield Independent Health and Water Commission, administered by City X University and Valley Environment Watch with no financial relationship to the operator or government. It provides quarterly public health and water reports, publishes test results within 14 days, offers free occupational screening for informal workers within 60 days, supplies safe water immediately, and uses no-fault compensation without assigning legal liability for pre-2025 damage to the ministry, KMC, or the operator.

transcript_entries.speaker: Community Leader
transcript_entries.text: This sounds like a good start, but "no financial relationship" is too vague. We need to ensure the funding for this commission comes from a dedicated, secure source, not just whatever is left over.

transcript_entries.speaker: National Government
transcript_entries.text: Propose on Livelihood protections: Formally recognize SWCA as an implementation negotiating party. Existing informal workers receive guaranteed recyclable access or equivalent paid sorting contracts for 24 months, access to the NPR 25M transition fund, training, crop-yield compensation for affected households, and a named worker grievance mechanism with a 14-day response deadline. Implementation is delegated to KMC, but KMC carries no direct financial obligation.

transcript_entries.speaker: Informal Sector Worker
transcript_entries.text: The proposal for SWCA recognition and 24 months of access is a start, but "implementation delegated to KMC" with no direct financial obligation leaves us vulnerable. We need to see that financial commitment clearly laid out.

transcript_entries.speaker: National Government
transcript_entries.text: Propose on Monitoring and accountability: Create the Eastfield Independent Oversight Board with City X University, Valley Environment Watch, one community-nominated representative, one SWCA representative, KMC coordination, and a public escalation pathway with a 30-day response deadline. The board can publish findings and recommend penalties, but cannot halt company operations without 30 days written notice plus national ministerial sign-off, and cannot issue operational directives to KMC without 30 days written notice.

transcript_entries.speaker: NGO / Civil Society
transcript_entries.text: The proposed board structure is a reasonable starting point, but the clause regarding operational directives needs refinement. We require the board to have the authority to issue direct operational directives to KMC, not just recommendations, with a clear, expedited timeframe for compliance.

transcript_entries.text: "No legal admission of pre-2025 liability" - that's precisely what we fear will be used to dismiss our current suffering. We need to ensure the commission's funding is dedicated and secure, not subject to the whims of government budgets.

transcript_entries.text: Round 3 Amend on Monitoring and accountability: Add the 30-day community escalation pathway, 14-day worker grievance link, 30 days written notice before KMC directives, and ministerial sign-off before any operational halt.
